replies.author: @jacksimpson8529
replies.text:
Hulk being afraid of fighting after getting crushed by Thanos wasn't in and of itself a problem. It seemed like quite a natural response to a rather child-like (mentally) character who'd up till that point been all but unstoppable getting his ass handed to him in such a humiliating fashion by someone he'd never seen before. That was a great set-up for further development.

The problem was that it was all then thrown out, much like Hulk himself was. The writers never elaborated on or used any of it.

Hulk could've gotten the classic comeback arc where he regains his confidence in himself via Bruce and other Avengers talking him through it, until he comes roaring back and manages to do much better fighting Thanos a second time.

Or, at least, they could have used that to lead into Professor Hulk, with Bruce finally admitting to Hulk that they need each other more than ever. Hulk needs Bruce's intellect to apply his strength properly, while Bruce needs Hulk's strength to defend himself when his own talents fail him. This leads to them finally accepting each other as a part of the same being and then merging into Professor Hulk.

But what did we ACTUALLY get? Nothing. Hulk's gone. Next time we see Bruce, he's already Professor Hulk. Barely a line or two dropped to explain the integration, instead of showing it in a storyline or full movie involving Hulk. His character was completely wasted, metaphorically and literally, and that's why all the anger around Hulk.

I understand it was probably written by someone who's never even considered such things much less knew the Hulk's background, but within the context of the story it was inexcusable.

replies.text:
The real problem is that:

#1. Disney bought up other IP meaning that their problem has automatically spread to them.

#2. Other Hollywood studios and streaming content producers followed suit.

Disney might finally see the light, but the momentum of the changes THEY made will take much longer to dissipate within the wider industry.

For my part I haven't been to the cinema at all in 5 years, and I have no plans to do so no matter what comes on.

Maybe in another 5 years I might go back if they all start cleaning up their act now.

But more than likely by that time VR headsets will be good enough to almost entirely replace the cinema theater experience with one where you don't have to share a room with a couple hundred strangers for 2 hours without the ability to stop the film and use a toilet.

In short they missed the boat - they saw declining attendance per capita and instead of diversifying content that men and women separately WANT to watch they tried to force the content for one gender to conform to another.

5-10 years from now it will be possible to get a moderately affordable VR/AR headset that is both light and high enough quality for HD+ movie watching - if some enterprising company makes a cinema theater app for VR that allows for social media style multiplayer interaction within such an app for "watch along" experiences then it's a foregone conclusion that physical cinema will be done for.

I'm a female who liked fantasy and sci-fi for as long as I can remember (in my thirties now).
Honestly I probably did miss some aspiring female protagonists in my childhood. Some titles even made me angry when I was older by depicting almost every woman as silly, unimportant and week, not worth of being respected or listened to. Men now often forget that it was like that for women for so very long.
But I generally agree with the points made, because
1. I did not ask for girl bosses, I asked for some brave, complex and capable women characters, who can also go on adventure. Maybe even save the day sometimes.
2. I absolutely did NOT ask to remove strong men characters. In fact, I love them.
Gender shouldn't really matter. Good stories, interesting characters, and a bit of inspiration for both boys and girls should matter.

replies.author: @nevarelixe3963
replies.text:
@felixmalcherek2919 It's a fundamentally flawed argument. The Romans became Nicene Christians and thus... were conquered by the Arian Christian Visigoths, Ostrogoths, and Vandals? It's not an internally consistent approach to the wider collapse of the empire. One can reasonably place some of the blame on Christianity, in that it introduced internal conflict over religion that had not existed, by and large, before, but that would also ignore the large list of issues the empire faced.

Such as: the increasing proliferation of Germanic populations that were failing to assimilate, the increase of non-Romans among leadership, the exorbitant cost of Rome's "Bread and Circus" strategy within the city itself, the failure to develop regional capitals (mostly due to...), the recurring issue of regional governors declaring themselves Emperor (Three of which came from Britain _alone_), the hyper inflation, the corruption of the Praetorians, the vertical split of the empire leaving the majority of economic wealth in the east, the ravages of Atilla the Hun,  and any of a myriad other issues.

To blame Christianity alone is an overly narrow view that simply doesn't hold water.

My intention is not to bash your teacher when I say this. There was a large push from the 60's-90's to push for increasingly secular and even atheistic thought in higher education. This came at the expense of the academic integrity of the work and can be seen in "The End of History" by Francis Fukuyama. Especially after the fall of the USSR, the west believed that we had hit the final evolution of government. Liberal Democracy with a capitalistic economic system would be the final form of government. That, like Japan, the 3rd world would see the wealth of the 1st world and come to embody our cultural values. Under such a system, the differences between the different religious sects of Europe had become unimportant background information. Theological debates that didn't stand in the way of economic or political cooperation between Catholic France, Protestant England, and Orthodox Greece. And thus religion largely came to be considered by academia a relic of a bygone era. And so it came to be blamed for the perceived "failure" to arrive at this end of governmental evolution earlier.


Overall, it's a LARGE topic; both why Rome fell and why "it's Christianity's fault!" was such a rampant (yet wrong) interpretation.

It's the same thing. Yes, you could just go to another fast food place. There are others that exist in America. But that doesn't change the fact that you are annoyed at such a big chain taking a stance that seems designed to piss you off.

The reason why Critical Drinker and company mostly produce negative videos that portray movies as piles of garbage is because most movies today are piles of garbage. He does have videos on movies that were good, or even just "okay". He says over and over again in these videos [paraphrased]: "I don't expect every single movie to be an unparalleled cinematic masterpiece. Sometimes I just want to be entertained." 
If you have watched enough of his videos, then you would realize that he is not negative all the time. If a movie is good, or even adequate, or even has anything positive in it at all, he will mention it. The only way somebody could only call him biased in his critiques if they are biased themselves.

Also, he rarely makes the political side the focus of his video unless it is GLARINGLY obvious that a political grudge is making a movie into a pile of garbage. He focuses mainly on the things other than politics which make a movie bad - poor actors, bad script, shallow characters, annoying cinematography (e.g. the constant lens flares in the new Star Trek movie), hammy/overwrought acting (e.g. the new Star Trek movie when Spock sacrifices himself), stupid costume / set choices, bad character design... etcetera, etcetera, etcetera.
If Critical Drinker only focused on politics in movies to the exclusion of all else, __he would not be as popular as he is today.__

This was a thing throughout my early childhood, too, and I'm 33. You'd see elements of it in real life, particularly in regards to social behaviour (I always felt like the implication was that girls were far more socially competent). For example, it was always 'what did the boy do to the girl to make her upset', especially when we got into our teens and started dating. 

It ties into the 'Women-are-wonderful effect' (there's an article on wikipedia with a good summary), which suggests that people associate more positive attributes with women when compared to men.

Back then you didn't bat an eyelid because it seemed to be part of the vaguely understood gender dynamics that'd developed over the previous decades, and it never felt particularly malicious.

author: @redgrizzly1492
text: I've been watching and rewatching alot of movies from the 90s lately. One thing that stuck with me is that the characters (both male and female) looked, sounded, and felt like adults. So much of modern mainstream media now portray their characters as whiny, infantilized adult-sized children. Watch De Niro and Pacino in Heat. Bruce Willis and Sam Jackson in Die Hard 3. And then watch anything Disney/Marvel has released in the last 5 years. John McClane looked and acted like your dad who made way too little money but still tried his best to keep it together and you loved him for it warts and all. Now watch anything Disney/Marvel have done in the last 5 years or so and you tell me if any of those characters can go 5 minutes without breaking down into absolute hysterics over the car breaking down, or the rent being due, or anything that doesn't offer them the validation and instant gratification that they so desperately crave. Granted, these aren't 1 to 1 comparisons, but it speaks to a larger problem that goes beyond Disney's tone deaf approach: studios either can't or won't write about actual people anymore. The everyman on screen is dead because the people writing for these movies live in these insulated, privileged little bubbles that know nothing about the struggles of the common people. They wanted to brand everything they found "coarse and boorish" about men (and women too) as toxic behaviour that needs to be corrected without realizing that alot of that was just how normal, everyday working class people have always behaved and acted (for good or ill), and they are incapable of understanding why. You always fear and hate what you don't understand. That lack of worldy knowledge has unfortunately poisoned the writers' room of almost every major studio in Hollywood so you end up with what we have now: characters who are whiny, overly neurotic, incompetent pansies who still somehow find it in themselves to lecture everybody else on how to go about their day. It's sad. So sad. It's a sad, sad situation that's only getting more and more absurd

author: @PyroMancer2k
text:
What Disney did to Deadpool where they took all that money and put it towards their woke projects rather than putting it back into an IP that fans actually like is proof they don't care about "getting men back" they just care about parting them with their money. As such Boycott anything Disney forever because like an abusive spouse they will NEVER change. Even if they do manage to pull off something like Deadpool it'll just be used to feed their own agenda driven projects. 

Despite what they want you to think the people are the ones with the power. A great example of this back during the actual civil rights movement when there really was discrimination. Most people are taught a pro-government story regarding Rosa Parks. I know in my school they taught how she was removed from the bus for sitting in the white section and their was such public outrage that the government had to step in and change the laws making it illegal. This is not what really happen and paints the government as the ones who need to step in to make things right. 

What REALLY happen was the people were outrage and so they formed a Boycott to the bus company. To help each other out they carpooled for people without cars and generally all came together to stop using the bus services. The bus company was losing so much money that they caved and were forced to repel the policy themselves. So the people through financial pressure on a company got them to change their policy. The government which moves extremely slowly didn't get around to pass a law to make it illegal until many months later when it wasn't even really an issue anymore but like every politician they were more than happy to claim credit for it. 

The main problem with similar tactics in today's world though is a lot of these companies are so massive they can absorb the losses of a few boycotts for a while. Thus gotta prepare for a decade or more long boycott to bring a company down. And gotta stay strong as Cash infuses like the success of Deadpool 3 can keep them going longer. These companies are so large that sure while it's great to see they are "struggling" they aren't near bankruptcy and likely to keep rolling along for many years before they are actually in serious financial troubles.

For me, I do have the issue that the friend who really liked to watch movies in the theater has moved far enough away that we can't really just do the movie thing anymore and doing it by myself is kinda just an "ok" experience.

But, ultimately, except for MAYBE Spider-man, I don't really care anymore for Marvel.  Star Wars is a little better in the sense that I still have SWTOR as well as many of the older Star Wars games but, except for the second season of Andor (which I'll probably see in December), I'm just past it.

And there is the issue.  Newton said that an object at rest tends to stay at rest and an object in motion tends to stay in motion.  Now that a large portion of the fanbase have become "meh" about the brand, even if Disney is sincere, it will be a herculean effort to get the object back in motion.

Can it be done?  Maybe.  While few things are impossible, between the sheer apathy towards the brand and the other headwinds (Movie sales have still not reached 2017-2019 levels) running against it, the odds aren't good.

replies.text:
I was born in 1997 and this video expertly sums up the problems of modern day entertainment (especially from Disney) and how utterly asinine these companies have been to the male audiences who made them successful for years. They abandoned the original creators and fans (who were overwhelmingly MEN), told them they didn't matter, desecrated their franchises while blaming them for not enjoying them and then chased after the "cool mainstream liberal" crowds they apparently cared about more...who never really liked their content to begin with and have long since moved on. Those people? They only cared about Star Wars and Marvel because it was fashionable and profitable at the time...and they could use them to insert their agendas and gain industry clout. Now that it's far less popular or profitable for them, they're disassociating from these IPs because they were never fans...they were producers, celebrities, activists, opportunists and other hacks.

We never said we didn't want female and diverse characters in our movies and shows. SW and Marvel have always had amazing female characters and characters of color. The issue is that these companies have skewed so far in the other direction in their attempts to appeal to an illusive, imaginary audience (which includes women) that were not and will never be interested in this stuff. And that's fine. Men like different things than women, like Drinker said. Some women are interested, but they constitute an infinitesimal percentage of the fanbase that will NEVER eclipse the amount of male fans.

Now they have practically no one left to consume their media and it's going to take a great deal of effort to restore their reputations in fans' eyes. This is a classic case of karma and it's extremely cathartic after being inundated with this bullshit for years. Hopefully it'll lead to real, meaningful change in the industry, but I'll believe it when I see it...otherwise, like everyone else, I refuse to support anything else they produce from now on. Please, Disney (and every company)...get your shit together and give us quality entertainment again instead of all the woke crap.

author: @joelt2002
text:
They have a serious brand problem. It's not that they killed their Golden Goose (they did). It's not that they need to start to produce good movies (they do). It's that Disney quite literally created the perception that they hate their customers. Who wants to buy products from a company that hates them? I sure don't. At first we thought it was just woke little social justice warriors within the company causing problems. But it was obvious that Bob Igor and the share holders were fully onboard with not only hating men but hating families. Sexualizing children. 

That is core rot. Not something easily cleaned. The entire company needs to sanitize itself and rebuild its image else it's pretty much headed towards corporate acquisition. You would need a new CEO that comes in and just starts firing large portions of the organization. A major shake up to show that they mean business about returning to their roots.

I don't see the current Disney having the balls to do that. Maybe they can just start producing good movies again and hope people forget what they have done. It's possible in a decade people will forget about this last decade.
